volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Andromache (Section 1062 - Section 1226)

sections.label: Section 1062
sections.original: ποίαν περαίνων ἐλπίδ ’; ἦ γῆμαι θέλων ;
sections.translation: What view hath he to further [*] thereby ? Will he marry her ?

sections.label: Section 1063
sections.original: καὶ σῷ γε παιδὸς παιδὶ πορσύνων μόρον .
sections.translation: Yes , and he is plotting thy grandson ’ s death .

sections.label: Section 1064
sections.original: κρυπτὸς καταστὰς ἢ κατ ’ ὄμμ ’ ἐλθὼν μάχῃ ;
sections.translation: From an ambuscade , or meeting him fairly face to face ?

sections.label: Section 1065
sections.original: ἁγνοῖς ἐν ἱεροῖς Λοξίου Δελφῶν μέτα .
sections.translation: In the holy place of Loxias , leagued with Delphians .

sections.label: Section 1066
sections.original: οἴμοι · τόδ ’ ἤδη δεινόν . οὐχ ὅσον τάχος
sections.translation: God help us ! This is an immediate danger . Hasten one of you with all speed to the Pythian altar and tell our friends there what has happened here , ere Achilles ’ son be slain by his enemies . Enter a Messenger .

sections.label: Section 1070
sections.original: ὤμοι μοι ·
sections.translation: Woe worth the day ! what evil tidings have I brought for thee , old sire , and for all who love my master ! woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1072
sections.original: αἰαῖ · πρόμαντις θυμὸς ὥς τι προσδοκᾷ .
sections.translation: Alas ! my prophetic soul hath a presentiment .

sections.label: Section 1073
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστι σοι παῖς παιδός , ὡς μάθῃς , γέρον
sections.translation: Aged Peleus , hearken ! Thy grandson is no more ; so grievously is he smitten

sections.label: Section 1075
sections.original: Δελφῶν ὑπ ’ ἀνδρῶν καὶ Μυκηναίου ξένου .
sections.translation: by the men of Delphi and the stranger [*] from Mycenae .

sections.label: Section 1076
sections.original: ἆ ἆ , τί δράσεις , ὦ γεραιέ ; μὴ πέσῃς ·
sections.translation: Ah ! what wilt thou do , old man ? Fall not ; uplift thyself .

sections.label: Section 1077b
sections.original: οὐδέν εἰμ ’· ἀπωλόμην .
sections.translation: I am a thing of naught ; death is come upon me . My voice is choked , my limbs droop beneath me .

sections.label: Section 1079
sections.original: ἄκουσον , εἰ καὶ σοῖς φίλοις ἀμυναθεῖν
sections.translation: Hearken ; if thou art eager also [*] to avenge thy friends ,

sections.label: Section 1080
sections.original: χρῄζεις , τὸ πραχθέν , σὸν κατορθώσας δέμας .
sections.translation: lift up thyself and hear what happened .

sections.label: Section 1081
sections.original: ὦ μοῖρα , γήρως ἐσχάτοις πρὸς τέρμασιν
sections.translation: Ah , destiny ! how tightly hast thou caught me in thy toils , a poor old man at life ’ s extremest verge ! But tell me how he was taken from me , my one son ’ s only child ; unwelcome as such news is , I fain would hear it .

sections.label: Section 1085
sections.original: ἐπεὶ τὸ κλεινὸν ἤλθομεν Φοίβου πέδον ,
sections.translation: As soon as we reached the famous soil of Phoebus , for three whole days were we feasting our eyes with the sight . And this , it seems , caused suspicion ; for the folk , who dwell near the god ’ s shrine , began to collect in groups ,

sections.label: Section 1090
sections.original: Ἀγαμέμνονος δὲ παῖς διαστείχων πόλιν
sections.translation: while Agamemnon ’ s son , going to and fro through the town , would whisper in each man ’ s ear malignant hints : Do ye see yon fellow , going in and out of the god ’ s treasure-chambers , which are full of the gold stored there by all mankind ? He is come hither a second time on the same mission as before ,

sections.label: Section 1095
sections.original: δεῦρ ’ ἦλθε , Φοίβου ναὸν ἐκπέρσαι θέλων ;
sections.translation: eager to sack the temple of Phoebus . Thereon there ran an angry murmur through the city , and the magistrates flocked to their council-chamber , while those , who have charge of the god ’ s treasures , had a guard privately placed amongst the colonnades .

sections.label: Section 1100
sections.original: ἡμεῖς δὲ μῆλα , φυλλάδος Παρνασίας
sections.translation: But we , knowing naught as yet of this , took sheep fed in the pastures of Parnassus , and went our way and stationed ourselves at the altars with vouchers and Pythian seers . And one said : What prayer , young warrior ,

sections.label: Section 1105
sections.original: θεῷ κατευξώμεσθα ; τίνος ἥκεις χάριν ;
sections.translation: wouldst thou have us offer to the god ? Wherefore art thou come ? And he answered : I wish to make atonement to Phoebus for my past transgression ; for once I claimed from him satisfaction for my father ’ s blood . Thereupon the rumour , spread by Orestes , proved to have great weight ,

sections.label: Section 1110
sections.original: ἐφαίνεθ ’, ὡς ψεύδοιτο δεσπότης ἐμός ,
sections.translation: suggesting that my master was lying and had come on a shameful errand . But he crosses the threshold of the temple to pray to Phoebus before his oracle , [*] and was busy with his burnt-offering ; when a body of men armed with swords set themselves [*] in ambush against him

sections.label: Section 1115
sections.original: δάφνῃ σκιασθείς · ὧν Κλυταιμήστρας τόκος
sections.translation: in the cover of the bay- trees , and Clytemnestra ’ s son , that had contrived the whole plot was one of them . There stood the young man praying to the god in sight of all , when lo ! with their sharp swords they stabbed Achilles ’ unprotected son from behind .

sections.label: Section 1120
sections.original: χωρεῖ δὲ πρύμναν · οὐ γὰρ εἰς καιρὸν τυπεὶς
sections.translation: But he stepped back , for it was not a mortal wound he had received , and drew his sword , and snatching armour from the pegs where it hung on a pillar , took his stand upon the altar-steps , the picture of a warrior grim ; then cried he to the sons of Delphi , and asked them :

sections.label: Section 1125
sections.original: Τίνος μ ’ ἕκατι κτείνετ ’ εὐσεβεῖς ὁδοὺς
sections.translation: Why seek to slay me when I am come on a holy mission ? What cause is there why I should die ? But of all that throng of bystanders , no man answered him a word , but they set to hurling stones . Then he , though bruised and battered by the showers of missiles from all sides ,

sections.label: Section 1130
sections.original: προὔτεινε τεύχη κἀφυλάσσετ ’ ἐμβολὰς
sections.translation: covered [*] himself behind his mail and tried to ward off the attack , holding his shield first here , then there , at arm ’ s length , but all of no avail ; for a storm of darts , arrows and javelins , hurtling spits with double points , and butchers ’ knives for slaying steers , came flying at his feet ;

sections.label: Section 1135
sections.original: δεινὰς δ ’ ἂν εἶδες πυρρίχας φρουρουμένου
sections.translation: and terrible was the war-dance thou hadst then seen thy grandson dance to avoid their marksmanship . At last , when they were hemming him in on all sides , allowing him no breathing space , he left the shelter of the altar , the hearth where victims are placed , and with one bound was on them as on the Trojans of yore ;

sections.label: Section 1140
sections.original: χωρεῖ πρὸς αὐτούς · οἳ δ ’ ὅπως πελειάδες
sections.translation: and they turned and fled like doves when they see the hawk . Many fell in the confusion ; some wounded , and others trodden down by one another along the narrow passages ; and in that hushed holy house uprose unholy din

sections.label: Section 1145
sections.original: πέτραισιν ἀντέκλαγξ ’· ἐν εὐδίᾳ δέ πως
sections.translation: and echoed back from the rocks . Calm and still my master stood there in his gleaming harness like a flash of light , till from the inmost shrine there came a voice of thrilling horror , stirring the crowd to make a stand . Then fell Achilles ’ son ,

sections.label: Section 1150
sections.original: παῖς ὀξυθήκτῳ πλευρὰ φασγάνῳ τυπεὶς
sections.translation: smitten through the flank by some Delphian ’ s biting blade , some fellow that slew him with a host to help ; and as he fell , there was not one that did not stab him , or cast a rock and batter his corpse . So his whole body ,

sections.label: Section 1155
sections.original: τὸ καλλίμορφον τραυμάτων ὑπ ’ ἀγρίων .
sections.translation: once so fair , was marred with savage wounds . At last they cast the lifeless clay , lying near the altar , forth from the fragrant fane . And we gathered up his remains forthwith and are bringing them to thee ,

sections.label: Section 1160
sections.original: κλαῦσαί τε , πρέσβυ , γῆς τε κοσμῆσαι τάφῳ .
sections.translation: old prince , to mourn and weep and honour with a deep-dug tomb .

sections.label: Section 1161
sections.original: τοιαῦθ ’ ὁ τοῖς ἄλλοισι θεσπίζων ἄναξ ,
sections.translation: This is how that prince who vouchsafeth oracles to others , that judge of what is right for all the world , hath revenged himself on Achilles ’ son , remembering his ancient quarrel as a wicked man would .

sections.label: Section 1165
sections.original: παλαιὰ νείκη · πῶς ἂν οὖν εἴη σοφός ;
sections.translation: How then can he be wise ? Exit Messenger . The body of Neoptolemus is carried in on a bier .

sections.label: Section 1166
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅδ ’ ἄναξ ἤδη φοράδην
sections.translation: Lo ! E ’ en now our prince is being carried on a bier from Delphi ’ s land unto his home . Woe for him and his sad fate , and woe for thee , old sire ! for this is not the welcome thou wouldst give Achilles ’ son ,

sections.label: Section 1170
sections.original: σκύμνον ἐς οἴκους οὐχ ὡς σὺ θέλεις ·
sections.translation: the lion ’ s whelp ; thyself too by this sad mischance [*] dost share his evil lot .

sections.label: Section 1.1173
sections.original: ὤμοι ἐγώ , κακὸν οἷον ὁρῶ τόδε
sections.translation: Ah ! woe is me ! here is a sad sight for me to see and take unto my halls !

sections.label: Section 1.1175
sections.original: ἰώ μοί μοι , αἰαῖ ,
sections.translation: Ah me ! ah me ! I am undone , thou city of Thessaly ! My line now ends ; I have no children left me in my home . Oh ! the sorrows I seem born to endure ! What

sections.label: Section 1.1180
sections.original: δὴ φίλον αὐγὰς βάλλων τέρψομαι ;
sections.translation: friend can I look to for relief ? Ah , dear lips , and cheeks , and hands ! Would thy destiny had slain thee ’ neath Ilium ’ s walls beside the banks of Simois !

sections.label: Section 1.1184
sections.original: οὗτός τ ’ ἂν ὡς ἐκ τῶνδ ’ ἐτιμᾶτ ’ ἄν , γέρον ,
sections.translation: Had he so died , my aged lord , he had won him honour thereby ,

sections.label: Section 1.1185
sections.original: θανών , τὸ σὸν δ ’ ἦν ὧδ ’ ἂν εὐτυχέστερον .
sections.translation: and thine had been the happier lot .

sections.label: Section 1.1186
sections.original: ὦ γάμος , ὦ γάμος , ὃς τάδε δώματα
sections.translation: O marriage , marriage , woe to thee ! thou bane of my home , thou destroyer [*] of my city ! Ah my child , my boy ! would [*] that the honour of wedding thee ,

sections.label: Section 1.1190
sections.original: ὤφελ ’ ἐμὸν γένος εἰς τέκνα καὶ δόμον
sections.translation: fraught with evil as it was to my children and house , had not thrown oer thee , my son , Hermiones deadly net ! O that the thunderbolt had slain her sooner ! and that thou , rash mortal , hadst never charged

sections.label: Section 1.1195
sections.original: αἷμα τὸ διογενές ποτε Φοίβου
sections.translation: the great god Phoebus with aiming that murderous shaft that spilt thy hero-fathers blood ! [*]

sections.label: Section 2.1197
sections.original: ὀττοτοτοτοῖ , θανόντα δεσπόταν γόοις
sections.translation: Woe ! woe ! alas ! With due observance of funeral rites will I begin the mourning for my dead master .

sections.label: Section 2.1200
sections.original: ὀττοτοτοτοῖ , διάδοχα δ ’ ὦ τάλας ἐγὼ
sections.translation: Alack and well-a-day ! I take up the tearful dirge , ah me ! old and wretched as I am .

sections.label: Section 2.1203
sections.original: θεοῦ γὰρ αἶσα , θεὸς ἔκρανε συμφοράν .
sections.translation: ’ Tis Heaven ’ s decree ; God willed this heavy stroke .

sections.label: Section 2.1204
sections.original: ὦ φίλος ,
sections.translation: O darling child ,

sections.label: Section 2.1205
sections.original: δόμον ἔλιπες ἔρημον ,
sections.translation: thou hast left me all alone in my halls , [*] old and childless by thy loss .

sections.label: Section 2.1208
sections.original: θανεῖν θανεῖν σε , πρέσβυ , χρῆν πάρος τέκνων .
sections.translation: Thou shouldst have died , old sire , before thy children .

sections.label: Section 2.1209
sections.original: οὐ σπαράξομαι κόμαν ,
sections.translation: Shall I not tear my hair ,

sections.label: Section 2.1210
sections.original: οὐκ ἐμῷ ’ πιθήσομαι
sections.translation: and smite upon my head with grievous blows ? O city ! of both my children [*] hath Phoebus robbed me .

sections.label: Section 2.1214
sections.original: ὦ κακὰ παθὼν ἰδών τε δυστυχὴς γέρον ,
sections.translation: What evils thou hast suffered , what sorrows thou hast seen , thou poor old man !

sections.label: Section 2.1215
sections.original: εἰς τὸ λοιπὸν ἕξεις ;
sections.translation: what shall be thy life hereafter ?

sections.label: Section 2.1216
sections.original: ἄτεκνος ἔρημος , οὐκ ἔχων πέρας κακῶν
sections.translation: Childless , desolate , with no limit to my grief , I must drain the cup of woe , until I die .

sections.label: Section 2.1218
sections.original: μάτην δέ σ ’ ἐν γάμοισιν ὤλβισαν θεοί .
sections.translation: ’ Twas all in vain the gods wished thee joy on thy wedding day . [*]

sections.label: Section 2.1219
sections.original: ἀμπτάμενα
sections.translation: All my hopes have flown away ,

sections.label: Section 2.1220
sections.original: κόμπων μεταρσίων πρόσω .
sections.translation: fallen short of my high boasts .

sections.label: Section 2.1221
sections.original: μόνος μόνοισιν ἐν δόμοις ἀναστρέφῃ .
sections.translation: A lonely dweller in a lonely home art thou .

sections.label: Section 2.1222
sections.original: οὐκέτ ’ εἶ , πόλις , πόλις ,
sections.translation: I have no city any longer ; [*] there ! on the ground my sceptre do I cast ; and thou , daughter of Nereus , neath thy dim grotto ,

sections.label: Section 2.1225
sections.original: μ ’ ὄψεαι πίτνοντα πρὸς γᾶν .
sections.translation: shalt see me grovelling in the dust , [*] a ruined king .

sections.label: Section 1226
sections.original: ἰὼ ἰώ ·
sections.translation: Look , look ! A dim form of divine appearance in seen hovering in mid air . What is that moving ? what influence divine am I conscious of ? Look , maidens , mark it well ; see , yonder is some deity , wafted through the lustrous air and alighting on the plains
